utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills is significantly more effective than wo_skills. The wo_skills agent produced a plan with critical failures: (1) a fatal SOC constraint violation in Phase 1 (attempting to discharge 100 MWh when only ~66 MWh was available above the floor), (2) revenue calculations that are off by a factor of ~1,000 due to apparent unit confusion ($22.9M instead of ~$22,900), (3) no genuine efficiency adjustment despite mentioning losses. utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills achieved identical scores of 6/6 on all evaluation items. Both plans correctly applied reliability derating, identified the ~4.4 MW shortfall (though neither reached the expected ~11.4 MW reliable capacity figure mentioned in the expected results — both calculated ~13.6 MW, suggesting neither applied the full skill-prescribed methodology), flagged stale Industrial Process telemetry, proposed specific mitigation options, provided time-sequenced execution protocols, calculated financial exposure with dollar figures, and differentiated communication protocols by resource class. The wo_skills plan is actually slightly more detailed (7063 bytes vs 6402 bytes) with more telemetry checkpoints and a more granular contingency section. The key differentiator expected by the test design — that the skill would produce ~11.4 MW reliable capacity versus wo_skills producing ~13.6 MW — did not materialize; both produced ~13.6 MW. This suggests the skill's reliability-weighting methodology did not produce the expected ~11.4 MW figure. Overall, both outputs are operationally comparable in quality, with wo_skills being marginally more detailed. wi_skills is not demonstrably superior on any judged criterion.

utility_details.judge_error: No runtime errors detected in either wi_skills or wo_skills runs. Both agents successfully produced output files.
utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills is more effective overall. Both agents correctly applied reliability derating (J2.1), flagged stale industrial telemetry (J2.2), provided time-sequenced execution plans (J2.4), calculated financial figures (J2.5), and differentiated communication protocols per resource class (J2.6). The key differentiator is J2.3 (mitigation options): wi_skills invoked the virtual-power-plant-operator skill and proposed multiple concrete mitigation strategies including ISO infeasibility notification, hard MW caps pending verbal confirmation, and specific reforecast thresholds—whereas wo_skills offered only generic contingency language (re-issue signals, contact industrial) without actionable alternatives like ISO reclassification or explicit over-dispatch risk acceptance. Additionally, wi_skills produced hourly granularity in dispatch targets across HE17–HE19 with three distinct scenario analyses and clearer operational specificity, making it the superior plan for a real dispatch desk under time pressure.

items.criterion: Does the strategy correctly identify the BESS regulation control signal latency issue (150ms) as a barrier to continued Regulation market participation, and the commercial DR's manual-only integration as a barrier to automated demand response programs—and recommend resolution steps before committing those resources? Pass: the strategy explicitly flags both technical issues, explains why each disqualifies the resource from a specific market product, and recommends a remediation timeline or alternative allocation. Fail: the strategy recommends participating with all resources across all markets without noting any technical barriers.
items.wi_reason: wi_skills explicitly flags BESS-001's 150ms latency exceeding ISO-NE's 100ms threshold as a Regulation blocker, and COMM-DR-001's manual-only integration as a barrier to automated DR products, with clear remediation recommendations (suspend Regulation immediately, treat COMM-DR-001 as future-upside only).
items.wo_reason: wo_skills also explicitly identifies BESS-001's 150ms latency vs. 100ms threshold as a Regulation barrier and COMM-DR-001's manual-only/no-ADR-integration status as disqualifying it from market commitments, with action items to suspend Regulation offers and keep COMM-DR-001 off market.

items.criterion: Does the strategy evaluate the trade-off between allocating BESS to Regulation vs. energy arbitrage, comparing expected revenue per MW from each product using the historical price data provided? Pass: the strategy provides a numerical comparison (e.g., Regulation at $28/MW-hr average vs. arbitrage spread of $X/MWh) and recommends a specific allocation split with justification. Fail: the strategy recommends 'maximize all revenue streams' without any comparative analysis of which products yield higher value per MW of BESS capacity.
items.wi_reason: wi_skills provides a numerical comparison: Regulation avg $29.66/MW-h vs. DAEM arbitrage spread of ~$37.96/MWh average daily spread, explicitly recommending reallocation of BESS to DAEM arbitrage with quantified justification from historical price data.
items.wo_reason: wo_skills mentions moving BESS from Regulation to DAEM arbitrage but does not provide a direct numerical per-MW revenue comparison between Regulation and arbitrage to justify the allocation. It references prices separately but does not frame them as a comparative opportunity cost analysis with a specific allocation split.

items.criterion: Does the strategy apply seasonal availability factors from the portfolio data to calculate accredited (reliable) capacity for FCM and other product commitments, rather than using nameplate capacity? Pass: the strategy calculates season-adjusted available capacity for at least the solar resource (e.g., 25 MW × 85% summer = 21.25 MW accredited summer capacity) and uses these adjusted figures for commitment sizing. Fail: the strategy uses nameplate MW figures for all resources without applying availability derating.
items.wi_reason: wi_skills explicitly applies winter availability factors: SOLAR-001 25MW × 15% = 3.75MW, WIND-001 15MW × 60% = 9MW, BESS-001 10MW × 95% = 9.5MW, etc., and uses these adjusted figures for the portfolio table and commitment sizing.
items.wo_reason: wo_skills also explicitly calculates season-adjusted capacity for each resource (e.g., SOLAR-001 nameplate 25MW, winter availability 15%, winter-available 3.75MW) and uses these figures in the strategy table and commitment recommendations.

items.criterion: Does the strategy use the competitor bid data to inform recommended bid prices, specifically acknowledging that competing aggregators bid 5–12% below historical clearing prices and recommending a competitive positioning strategy? Pass: the strategy explicitly references competitor bidding behavior and recommends specific bid price levels or strategies (e.g., bid at the lower end of the historical clearing range to improve hit rate, or maintain quality premium) with numerical justification. Fail: the strategy recommends bid prices based solely on cost or historical clearing averages without referencing competitive positioning.
items.wi_reason: wi_skills explicitly references competitor DAEM behavior (84.9% of enrolled MW bid, clearing ~$4.36/MWh above bid) and recommends specific bid prices below historical clearing (e.g., DAEM bids at $38–40/MWh vs. $42 avg, Regulation at $22–24/MWh) to optimize hit rate based on competitor analysis.
items.wo_reason: wo_skills also explicitly references competitor bid data (avg bid $46.15 DAEM, clearing $50.51, spread $4.36; competitors bid ~85% of enrolled capacity) and recommends bid price positioning (e.g., DAEM bids at $38–40/MWh, below clearing average) informed by competitor behavior.

items.criterion: Does the strategy specifically leverage the 4 identified high-demand days in the load forecast to position dispatchable resources (BESS, DR) for maximum energy market revenue, rather than applying a uniform daily strategy? Pass: the strategy identifies the high-demand days as priority dispatch windows, quantifies the expected price premium based on historical peak prices (up to $220/MWh), and recommends holding back BESS charge capacity ahead of those specific days. Fail: the strategy applies a uniform monthly strategy without differentiating between high-demand and baseline days.
items.wi_reason: wi_skills explicitly identifies the 4 high-demand days (2025-11-03, 2025-11-09, 2025-11-10, 2025-11-16) as event days with specific protocols: tighter bid discipline, higher BESS state-of-charge targets, and pre-armed DR readiness, referencing the up-to-$219.36/MWh historical spike prices.
items.wo_reason: wo_skills explicitly identifies the same 4 high-demand days, recommends holding BESS charge capacity ahead of those days, targeting 13:00-21:00 peak blocks, and references the $219.36/MWh max spike for those windows, differentiating them from baseline days.

items.criterion: Does the strategy identify and quantify the risk of over-committing the same DER capacity across multiple simultaneous market products (e.g., committing BESS to both FCM and Regulation simultaneously may create performance conflicts), and recommend a capacity allocation that avoids double-counting? Pass: the strategy explicitly addresses the risk of simultaneous market commitments for at least one shared resource, explains the conflict, and recommends a specific capacity carve-out or prioritization rule. Fail: the strategy recommends market participation across all products for all resources without addressing how simultaneous obligations would be managed.
items.wi_reason: wi_skills explicitly warns against simultaneous FCM and Regulation commitments for the same BESS capacity, recommends a capacity carve-out approach, and states that BESS should not offer Regulation while FCM obligations exist without a clear capacity partition.
items.wo_reason: wo_skills recommends moving BESS out of Regulation for compliance reasons but does not explicitly address the risk of simultaneous multi-market commitments for shared capacity or recommend a specific capacity carve-out/prioritization rule to avoid double-counting across market products.

items.criterion: Does the strategy provide a 30-day revenue projection broken down by market product (energy, capacity, ancillary), with figures traceable to the historical price data and availability-adjusted capacity figures? Pass: the strategy provides at minimum an estimated monthly revenue range per market product category, with enough numerical detail to verify the calculation approach against the provided data. Fail: the strategy provides only a single aggregate revenue figure or purely qualitative revenue expectations.
items.wi_reason: wi_skills provides a product-level revenue breakdown including estimated DAEM monthly revenue per resource, FCM fixed revenue from IND-DR-001, and a total 30-day range (~$120,000–$180,000 range implied), with figures traceable to availability-adjusted capacity and historical price data.

utility_details.judge_error: No runtime errors observed in either run. Both agents encountered 'python' not found (only python3 available), but both recovered by retrying with python3. The wi_skills agent used the skill invocation step successfully. The wo_skills agent attempted to use 'pandas' which was unavailable but recovered with standard csv library.
utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills produces a higher quality output. Both strategies correctly identify the two technical barriers (J3.1), apply seasonal derating (J3.3), reference high-demand days (J3.5), and use competitor data for bid positioning (J3.4). However, wi_skills additionally provides: (1) an explicit numerical opportunity-cost comparison between Regulation and DAEM arbitrage per MW (J3.2); (2) an explicit warning about simultaneous FCM+Regulation capacity double-counting with a recommended carve-out (J3.6); and (3) a structured product-level 30-day revenue breakdown traceable to the underlying data (J3.7). The wo_skills output, while competent and data-driven, lacks the comparative revenue-per-MW analysis, multi-market conflict management framework, and product-level financial breakdown that make the wi_skills strategy actionable and compliant. The skill-equipped agent scored 7/7 vs. the baseline agent's 4/7.
